metadata.title: IAAO Glossary

source.notes: Glossary terms support taxpayer-facing explanations, tooltips, and ratio-study standards context.

metadata.intendedUse: Guided Parcel Review glossary, tooltip, and standards-reference lookup

entries.definition: The value of a number or variable regardless of its sign.
The closeness of a measurement, computation, or estimate to the true, exact, or accepted value. Accuracy may also be expressed as a range around the true value.
The sale price after adjustments for time, personal property, financing, or other relevant factors.
The act or process of developing an opinion of value, or the resulting opinion of value.
In ad valorem taxation, the date defined by law for estimating a property's value.
The ratio of appraised value to an indicator of market value. By extension, an estimated fractional relationship between appraisals and market values for a group of properties.
A ratio study using independent expert single-property appraisals and/or sales based on market value as indicators of market value.
The ratio of appraised value to the sale price or adjusted sale price of a property; a simple indication of appraisal accuracy.
The estimated value of a property.
A measure of central tendency calculated by adding all values of a variable and dividing by the number of values.
An ordered arrangement of data, such as a listing of sales ratios in order of magnitude.
To determine the value of property for ad valorem taxation.
The value placed on property subject to taxation at market value or at a legally authorized fraction of market value.
The official act of determining the tax base. In property taxation, it includes discovering, listing, and appraising property. It may also refer to the value placed on property through that process.
The ratio of the assessed value of a property to an independent appraisal.
The status date for tax purposes. Appraised values reflect the condition, use, and status of the property as of this date.
An appraisal bias in which high-value properties are appraised higher or lower than low-value properties in relation to market values.

The fractional relationship of an assessed value to the market value of the property. By extension, the relationship of the total assessment roll to total market value of taxable property in a jurisdiction.
The ratio of assessed value to the sale price or adjusted sale price of a property.
A property tax official with professional responsibilities.
The arithmetic mean of the absolute deviations of a set of numbers from a measure of central tendency such as the median. Used in computing the coefficient of dispersion.
The tendency of a statistic to overestimate or underestimate a parameter.

A computer-intensive method of statistical inference based on repeated resampling of data, often used for confidence interval approximation when traditional formulas are unavailable or assumptions are not met.
See computer-assisted mass appraisal.
The tendency of data to cluster around a typical or central value, such as the mean or median. Also refers to statistics that describe that central value.

A set of items defined by common characteristics. In property taxation, examples include residential, agricultural, and industrial property. In statistics, it may refer to a predefined category for grouping data.
See coefficient of dispersion.
The percentage of observations within a specified percentage of a measure of central tendency, commonly used in assessment ratio studies.

The average deviation of a group of numbers from the median expressed as a percentage of the median. In ratio studies, it is the average percentage deviation from the median ratio and is used as a measure of appraisal uniformity.

An index of price-related bias obtained by regressing percentage deviations from the median ratio on percentage changes in a value proxy. It indicates the percentage by which assessment ratios change when values double or halve. The PRB should generally range between -0.05 and +0.05; values outside -0.10 to +0.10 are considered unacceptable.
A measure of relative variability calculated as the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean. Useful for comparing results between groups.

A software system used by governmental agencies and assessing offices to establish real and personal property valuations for tax purposes. It often includes statistical tools such as multiple regression analysis.
A range of values calculated from sample observations that is believed, with a stated probability, to contain the true population parameter.
The degree of probability associated with a statistical test or confidence interval, commonly 90, 95, or 99 percent.
The amount a component of a property contributes to total market value. For improvements, contributory value must be distinguished from cost.
See coefficient of variation.
The date on which the sale was consummated, typically the date the deed or instrument of transfer is signed.

The process of converting ratio study results into adjustment factors and changing locally determined appraised or assessed values to more nearly reflect market value or the legally required level of assessment.
The degree to which data are distributed tightly or loosely around a measure of central tendency.
Robust nonparametric methods whose interpretation or reliability does not depend on strict assumptions about the distribution of the underlying population.
The process by which a governmental body attempts to ensure that property under its jurisdiction is assessed at the same assessment ratio or at the ratio required by law.
The part of statistical practice concerned with reviewing data to isolate structures, uncover patterns, or reveal features that may improve confirmatory analysis.
An article of personal property installed or attached to real property in such a manner that it is considered part of the real property.
A percentage of full value as prescribed by law.
A table or chart showing the number or percentage of observations falling within a set of classes. Used in ratio studies to summarize individual ratios.
A bar chart or graph of a frequency distribution in which frequencies are shown by bars proportional to the number or percentage of observations in each class.
A statement in inferential statistics whose truth the analyst seeks to determine.
An estimate of value using a model different from that used for assessment purposes. Independent appraisals may supplement sales in sales ratio or appraisal ratio studies.
The process of computing hypothetical values that represent the oversight agency's best estimate of taxable value, given the legally required level of assessment or market value.
The result obtained by subtracting the first quartile from the third quartile. By definition, 50 percent of observations fall within the interquartile range.
A contract for sale of property in which the seller retains title until the buyer completes contracted payments.
The common or overall ratio of appraised values to market values. Relevant concepts include the legally required level, the true or actual level, and the computed level based on a ratio study.
The common or overall ratio of assessed values to market values. Relevant concepts include the legally required ratio, the true population ratio, and the estimated ratio based on a sample.
A measure of uncertainty associated with statistical estimates, commonly expressed as a plus-or-minus percentage or proportion at a specified confidence level.
A value opinion that presumes transfer of a property or property rights as of a certain date under specific conditions identified by the appraiser.
A property value estimate used as a stand-in for the unknown true market value of the property.
The process of valuing a universe of properties as of a given date using standard methodology, common data, and statistical testing.
See arithmetic mean.
A measure of central tendency. For an odd number of ordered items, the middle value; for an even number, the arithmetic average of the two central values.
The median of the absolute deviations from the median. In a symmetrical distribution, it approximates one-half the interquartile range.
The median of the absolute percent deviations from the median, calculated by dividing the median absolute deviation by one-hundredth of the median.
See distribution-free statistics.
Error in ratio study statistics from all sources other than sampling error. Practical steps should be taken to minimize it.

A theoretical symmetrical, bell-shaped distribution. In a normal distribution, about 68 percent of observations fall within one standard deviation of the mean, 95 percent within two, and 99 percent within three.
One recording or occurrence of the value of a variable, such as one sales ratio in a sample.
Observations with unusual values that differ markedly from a measure of central tendency.
A condition where a statistical model or machine learning algorithm becomes too complex for the data and captures noise or random variation rather than meaningful patterns.
A numerical descriptive measure of a population, such as the arithmetic mean or standard deviation. Parameters are generally unknown and estimated from sample statistics.
Statistics that assume sample data come from a population with a normal distribution.
Values that divide a set of ordered data into specified percentages.
All property not defined as real property. Generally includes inventories, fixed assets, leasehold improvements, and intangibles unless exempted by statute.
The incremental value increase that results when two or more sites are combined into one larger site with greater utility.
A single numerical value used to estimate a population parameter from a sample.
Prepaid interest on a loan. One point equals 1 percent of the loan amount.
All items of interest, such as all properties in a jurisdiction or all observations in a data set from which a sample may be drawn.
The level of detail in which a quantity or value is expressed, or the degree to which repeated measurements yield the same or nearly the same result.
See coefficient of price-related bias.
See price-related differential.
The amount asked, offered, or paid for a property.

A statistical measure of vertical property tax equity calculated by dividing the mean ratio by the weighted mean ratio. Values above 1.03 tend to indicate regressivity; values below 0.98 tend to indicate progressivity.
The unit of government having initial responsibility for determining assessed value for general property taxation.
See assessment progressivity (regressivity).
An aggregate of things or rights to things protected by law. The two basic types are real property and personal property.
A property transfer that satisfies the conditions of a valid sale and meets technical criteria for inclusion in a ratio study sample.
Values that divide an ordered data set into four equal parts.
A sample selected so each sample of the same size is equally likely, or each element has an equal chance of selection.
The maximum value of a sample minus the minimum value, or the difference between the maximum and minimum values a variable may assume.
A statistical study of the relationship between appraised or assessed values and market values, commonly using sale prices or independent appraisals as market value proxies.
The interests, benefits, and rights inherent in ownership of land plus anything permanently attached to land or legally defined as immovable.
See assessment progressivity (regressivity).
See price-related differential.
In sampling, the extent to which the process yields consistent population estimates. Representativeness, sample size, and sample uniformity contribute to reliability.
A sample of observations from a larger population such that sample statistics can be expected to represent the population being studied.
The actual amount of money exchanged for property, whether or not established in a free and open market. It may be an indicator of market value.
The ratio of an appraisal or assessed value to the sale price or adjusted sale price of a property.

The practice of using the sale of a property to trigger reappraisal of that property at or near the selling price. More broadly, any practice causing the analyzed sample to misrepresent assessment performance for the entire population.
A ratio study using sales prices as benchmarks for market values. It is used to evaluate assessment practices, reappraisals, or revaluations.
A subset containing characteristics of a larger population. A sample should represent the population as a whole and avoid bias toward a specific attribute.
A statistical error resulting from a sample that does not represent the population.
A graph depicting the relationship between two variables by plotting one variable on the horizontal axis and one on the vertical axis.
A measure of the probability that an event is attributable to a relationship rather than chance.
The quality of an asymmetrical frequency distribution. A distribution may be positively skewed or negatively skewed depending on the direction of its longer tail.
A statistic measuring dispersion around the mean. It is calculated from squared deviations from the mean and is most meaningful when data are normally distributed.
A measure of the precision of a measure of central tendency. Smaller standard errors indicate more reliable estimates.
The closeness between a statistical estimate and the true but unknown population parameter it was designed to measure. It can be decomposed into sampling and non-sampling error.
How close estimates from different samples are to each other. Precision is inversely related to standard error.
Numerical descriptive data calculated from a sample, such as the median, mean, or COD. Statistics are used to estimate population parameters.
To divide a sample into two or more subsets according to one or more criteria for analysis.
A class or subset resulting from stratification.
The sale price adjusted for market price changes between the date of sale and the date of analysis.
The arithmetic mean of a data set after a specified proportion of observations is trimmed from each end of the ordered array.

The relationship between an object desired and a potential owner, requiring scarcity, utility, desirability, and transferability. Also refers to the present worth of future benefits or the estimate sought in valuation.
An item of observation that can assume various values, such as square feet, sale prices, or sales ratios.
An average in which each value is adjusted by a factor reflecting its relative importance before values are summed and divided.
The sum of appraised values divided by the sum of sales prices or independent estimates of market value. This weights each ratio in proportion to sale price or market value estimate.
